items.title: Chronic insomnia and psychiatric comorbidity
items.contentHtml:
Chronic insomnia and psychiatric comorbidity

  • Chronic insomnia is defined by difficulty initiating or maintaining sleep for at least 3 nights/week for >3 months, with daytime consequences.
  • Up to 40% of patients with major depressive disorder present insomnia, and insomnia is an independent risk factor for developing depression.
  • First-line treatment It is cognitive-behavioral therapy for insomnia; drugs (eg zolpidem) are reserved for refractory or short-term cases.
  • Do not use benzodiazepines in the elderly due to the risk of falls and cognitive impairment; prefer ramelteon or doxepin at low doses.
  • Cheat trap: in insomnia with psychiatric comorbidity, treating insomnia first improves the prognosis of the underlying disorder.

items.title: Obstructive sleep apnea and polysomnography
items.contentHtml:
Obstructive sleep apnea and polysomnography

  • Obstructive sleep apnea is confirmed with polysomnography: apnea-hypopnea index (AHI) ≥5 events/hour plus symptoms (drowsiness, snoring) or AHI ≥15 without symptoms.
  • Polysomnography records electroencephalogram, electrooculogram, electromyogram, airflow, respiratory effort and oxygen saturation.
  • Key finding: decrease in oxygen saturation >3% associated with respiratory events.
  • First-line treatment is continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) in moderate to severe disease (AHI ≥15).
  • Cheating exam: polysomnography is not necessary if AHI is ≥30 with typical symptoms; but it is gold standard for diagnosis.

items.title: Restless legs syndrome and ferritin
items.contentHtml:
Restless legs syndrome and ferritin

  • restless legs syndrome is characterized by the urge to move the legs at rest, worsens in the evening/night and improves with movement.
  • Serum ferritin <75 ng/mL is associated with greater severity; Treatment with oral iron is indicated if ferritin <75 ng/mL or transferrin saturation <20%.
  • First pharmacological line: dopamine agonists (eg pramipexole) or gabapentin/pregabalin, avoiding levodopa due to risk of increase (augmentation).
  • Examination trap: do not start dopamine agonists if low ferritin without first correcting the iron deficiency.
  • Rule out uremia, peripheral neuropathy, iron deficiency and pregnancy as secondary causes.

items.title: Narcolepsy and cataplexy
items.contentHtml:
Narcolepsy and cataplexy

  • Narcolepsy type 1 presents with cataplexy (sudden loss of muscle tone due to emotions) and hypocretin-1 deficiency in cerebrospinal fluid.
  • Diagnosis requires polysomnography followed by multiple sleep latency testing: mean latency <8 minutes and ≥2 eye movement sleep episodes (REM) at the onset of sleep.
  • Cataplexy is pathognomonic of narcolepsy type 1; its first-line treatment is sodium oxybate (gamma-hydroxybutyrate).
  • For excessive daytime sleepiness: modafinil as first line, followed by amphetamines if necessary.
  • Examination trap: cataplexy is not loss of consciousness, just reversible muscle weakness.

items.title: REM sleep behavior disorder
items.contentHtml:
REM sleep behavior disorder

  • REM sleep behavior disorder is characterized by complex motor acts during REM sleep, associated with violent dreams, without muscle atonia.
  • It is confirmed with polysomnography: electromyogram shows excessive muscle activity during REM sleep.
  • Up to 80% of patients develop synucleinopathy (disease). Parkinson's disease, multiple system atrophy, dementia with Lewy bodies) in the long term (10-15 years).
  • First-line treatment: clonazepam at low doses (0.5-1 mg at bedtime), with caution in the elderly due to falls.
  • Alternative: melatonin (3-12 mg) if clonazepam is contraindicated.
  • Trap test: REM sleep behavior disorder is different from non-REM parasomnias, which occur in deep sleep.

items.foco: Excessive daytime sleepiness and Epworth scale
items.title: Excessive daytime sleepiness and Epworth scale
items.contentHtml:
Excessive daytime sleepiness and Epworth scale

  • The Epworth sleepiness scale evaluates the probability of falling asleep in 8 everyday situations (sitting reading, watching TV, etc.), score from 0 to 24.
  • Score >10 indicates excessive daytime sleepiness (EDS); >16 suggests severe somnolence.
  • SDE may be due to obstructive sleep apnea, narcolepsy, idiopathic hypersomnia, chronic sleep deprivation, or use of sedative drugs.
  • Differential diagnosis includes multiple sleep latency testing and actigraphy.
  • Cheating examination: the Epworth scale is subjective and does not replace polysomnography for etiology; In the elderly the score may be low despite objective SDE.

items.title: Sleep hygiene in insomnia
items.contentHtml:
Sleep hygiene in insomnia

  • Sleep hygiene includes regular bedtimes and wake-up times (even weekends), avoiding naps >30 minutes, and not using screens 1 hour before bed.
  • Avoid caffeine, nicotine, and alcohol at least 4-6 hours before bedtime; Alcohol fragments sleep in the second half of the night.
  • Keep the bedroom dark, cool and quiet; using the bed only for sleep and sexual intercourse.
  • Bed time restriction (reducing time in bed to actual sleep time) is part of cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia.
  • Cheat trap: sleep hygiene alone is insufficient for chronic insomnia; should be combined with cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia.

items.title: Melatonin and delayed phase disorder
items.contentHtml:
Melatonin and delayed phase disorder

  • Delayed sleep phase disorder occurs with a delay of >2 hours in the onset of sleep with respect to the desired time, with difficulty waking up.
  • Exogenous melatonin (0.5-3 mg) administered 1-2 hours before the desired bedtime helps to advance the phase circadian.
  • Chronotherapy (progressively delaying bedtime by 2-3 hours each day until the desired time is reached) is an alternative.
  • Extended-release melatonin (e.g. 2 mg) is approved for insomnia in people over 55 years of age in some countries.
  • Cheat trap: melatonin is not effective for sleep insomnia in young adults without circadian disorder; Its effect is modest.

items.title: Idiopathic hypersomnia vs narcolepsy
items.contentHtml:
Idiopathic hypersomnia vs. narcolepsy

  • Idiopathic hypersomnia presents with excessive daytime sleepiness, prolonged night sleep (>10 hours) and difficulty waking up (sleep inertia), without cataplexy.
  • In polysomnography, sleep latency is <8 minutes, but in the multiple sleep latency test there are <2 episodes of REM sleep at sleep onset (unlike narcolepsy).
  • Narcolepsy type 1 has cataplexy and ≥2 episodes of REM sleep at sleep onset; Type 2 narcolepsy does not have cataplexy but does have ≥2 episodes of REM sleep at sleep onset.
  • Treatment of idiopathic hypersomnia: modafinil or stimulants as first line, without response to sodium oxybate.
  • Cheat trap: idiopathic hypersomnia does not respond to restful naps (unlike narcolepsy).

items.title: Non-REM parasomnias (sleepwalking, night terrors)
items.contentHtml:
Non-REM parasomnias (sleepwalking, night terrors)

  • Non-REM parasomnias occur during slow-wave sleep (phases 3 and 4), usually in the first third of the night.
  • sleepwalking involves wandering with amnesia of the event; night terrors present with screaming, tachycardia and difficulty waking up, with no memory of the dream.
  • Precipitating factors: sleep deprivation, fever, alcohol, stress and drugs (zolpidem, lithium).
  • The diagnosis is clinical; Polysomnography is not routine unless there is violent behavior or diagnostic doubt.
  • Treatment: safety measures (close doors, remove dangerous objects), avoid waking the patient during the episode; if frequent, clonazepam at low doses (0.5-1 mg).
  • Examination trap: night terrors are different from nightmares (REM sleep, the patient remembers the content).

items.title: Pharmacological treatment of insomnia (zolpidem, ramelteon)
items.contentHtml:
Pharmacological treatment of insomnia (zolpidem, ramelteon)

  • Zolpidem (benzodiazepine receptor agonist, non-benzodiazepine) shortens sleep latency; short half-life (~2.5 h), risk of anterograde amnesia and sleepwalking.
  • Recommended dose: 5 mg in women and 5-10 mg in men, once a day, maximum 10 mg; avoid in the elderly due to falls.
  • Ramelteon (melatonin MT1/MT2 receptor agonist) is approved for sleep insomnia; has no potential for abuse or tolerance.
  • Alternatives: doxepin (3-6 mg) as a tricyclic antidepressant at hypnotic doses; suvorexant (dual orexin antagonist) for insomnia with difficulty maintaining sleep.
  • Check trap: chronic use of zolpidem (>4 weeks) is not recommended; ramelteon does not cause dependence and is safe in older people.

items.title: Apnea-hypopnea syndrome and CPAP
items.contentHtml:
Apnea-hypopnea syndrome and CPAP

  • Sleep apnea-hypopnea syndrome is defined by an apnea-hypopnea index (AHI) ≥5 with symptoms, or AHI ≥15 without symptoms.
  • Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) is the first-line treatment for disease. moderate to severe (AHI ≥15).
  • CPAP reduces excessive daytime sleepiness, improves blood pressure and reduces cardiovascular risk; optimal adherence is ≥4 hours/night.
  • Alternatives: mandibular advancement devices for mild-moderate apnea, surgery (uvulopalatopharyngoplasty) in selected cases with anatomical obstruction.
  • Cheating exam: CPAP does not cure apnea; if suspended, the events reappear. Weight loss can reduce AHI.
